item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: I'm fine with all of these.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Got a chance to spitball a few ideas for crosslinks for the good ol' Ghostlight with GreenWolf and came up with a few crosslinks. First off, the mentions of PL and the Factory can be crosslinked to their respective hubs. Additionally, clean up of broken instances of SCP-2176 likely would be cleaned up by MTF Mu-13, so a small reference can be made in the article there. A few articles that mention the foundation dealing with ghosts also link to PL's Electro-Thaumic Exorcists, so these could be the devices being used by Mu-13. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

A paratechnology company focused on consumer applications of anomalous technology, formed in 1999 by a number of former employees of Prometheus Laboratories.

To this:

A paratechnology company focused on consumer applications of anomalous technology, formed in 1999 by a number of former employees of Prometheus Laboratories.

Changing this:

Documents and testimony taken from TCAE representatives show that the ectoplasm used in SCP-2176 was sourced from a number of third-party suppliers, primarily the Group of Interest known as "The Factory" (GoI-010).

To this:

Documents and testimony taken from TCAE representatives show that the ectoplasm used in SCP-2176 was sourced from a number of third-party suppliers, primarily the Group of Interest known as "The Factory" (GoI-010).

Changing this:

Foundation involvement and containment efforts began in 2006, approximately six months after TCAE began selling SCP-2176 to consumers, following a rise in the number of household electrocutions. Once a causal link to SCP-2176 was established, the Foundation was able to convince TCAE to order a recall of the defective bulbs. Subsequent investigation revealed the presence of Class-A ectomorphs in approximately 40% of all SCP-2176 lightbulbs.

To this:

Foundation involvement and containment efforts began in 2006, approximately six months after TCAE began selling SCP-2176 to consumers, following a rise in the number of household electrocutions. Once a causal link to SCP-2176 was established, the Foundation was able to convince TCAE to order a recall of the defective bulbs. Subsequent investigation revealed the presence of Class-A ectomorphs in approximately 40% of all SCP-2176 lightbulbs. Ongoing containment is being performed by MTF Mu-13 ("Ghostbuster") through use of Hoffman Portable Electro-Thaumic Units.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know about anyone else, but for me I feel like this article would be better without using words such as "ectoplasm" and "ectomorph", instead referring to them as SCP-2176-1, SCP-2174-2. Provide enough information for the reader to realise that SCP-2176-1 is, in fact, ectoplasm, and the article becomes a bit more interesting and mysterious. At least, that's what I think. This just had too much of a Ghostbusters feel for me to see it as a proper SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just my two cents:

they reduce the wonder and terror of the Foundation universe

If I read this before getting interested in the foundation, I would be more likely to read more. Maybe it's just my love for investigative reports, but in my book, that's increasing the wonder of the SCP universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Isn't the entire point of the Foundation to take paranormal subjects and treat them with scientific rigor? … With this in mind, why wouldn't there be categorization and analysis of ghosts and demons and magic?

Yes, the Foundation attempts to take a scientific objective approach to anomalies. But if anomalies can be categorised according to a standard set of rules, then (a) in-universe, that doesn't seem particularly anomalous; and (b) as a reader, it's not as interesting, because the ghosts and demons are made, if not quite safe, then less unpredictable.

the existence of GOIs in general implies that there is a rather large segment of the human population that is aware of the existence of some paranormal concepts.

And I'm not a huge fan of this, or of PL as a result - my headcanon is that they are very small, and tend to sell only a few units for high value to other GOIs.

Admittedly, this is all in large part a headcanon thing on my part, and I have some flexibility, but I do think that there is a risk of taking the magic out of magic by enabling it to be listed and described in detail. I prefer a Foundation that is relatively small, has only mundane technology (with only a few minor exceptions like amnestics), and has to hold back the darkness based on ingenuity and courage alone.

I feel like the examples that Scorpion451 listed fit my point. These articles make it seem as though ghosts and demons are at least as well-understood as x-rays or Americium, so there doesn't seem much reason to keep *any* masquerade in relation to them. The Foundation seems quite arbitrary in what it considers anomalous, by this measure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I missed this when you first posted it, and Scorpion451 already addressed the issues you raised pretty well, but I'd like to respond to one part in particular:

they treat the use of paranormal concepts in wide-use technology as a mundane fact, accepted by the Foundation and subject to a high degree of categorisation and scientific analysis;

Isn't the entire point of the Foundation to take paranormal subjects and treat them with scientific rigor? Skips aren't just stories written in clinical tone, they're (in-universe) scientific documents of a research organization dedicated to analyzing and understanding things that go against what we know is possible. With this in mind, why wouldn't there be categorization and analysis of ghosts and demons and magic?

Additionally, the very existence of Groups of Interest like Prometheus Labs implies that there is a market for paratech1. If there wasn't, the company would not exist, because it would not have any customers

Hell, the existence of GOIs in general implies that there is a rather large segment of the human population that is aware of the existence of some paranormal concepts. The masquerade isn't a wall, it's a chain link fence.

Footnotes
1. A term that I use to mean both anomalous technology, and non-anomalous technology that is advanced beyond the current publicly-known state-of-the-art.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How did you guess, DreamwalkerFae?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My response upon seeing this:
"Ghosts in a lightbulb. Is this a GreenWolf thing? I bet this is a GreenWolf thing."
Checks history.
"Yep, GreenWolf thing."
Reads rest of the article. Upvotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My point is: if ghosts and demons are so well-understood that they can be categorised by type, and used in household applications, then are they even anomalous anymore? Why not just leak the details to science (calling them waves or particles or some other non-emotive name) and stop wasting money containing them?

Just because you can observe and classify a phenomena doesn't mean you understand it, just because you don't understand a phenomena doesn't mean you can't use it, and just because something is extremely dangerous doesn't mean it can't be used with complete safety given proper handling.

For comparison: The X-ray tube and fluoroscope were invented decades before we really understood what radiation was, how it worked, and what it did to people. A lot of intelligent, well educated, rational scientists, engineers, doctors, and inventors died because it never occurred to them that there was more to this device than a novel type of lightbulb until people started falling apart from radiation poisoning and cancer.

On the flip side, ionization-type smoke detectors contain tiny chips of weapons grade Americanium-241. This is perfectly safe, and nearly idiot proof. You could literally eat the pellet from one of these and live (Its not a good idea, but you could theoretically do it), and it would take tens of thousands of them to build even a small bomb.

Its all about acceptable levels of risk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm downvoting. This is one of a series of recent works (including 2336, 2167 and the PL article on computer exorcisms) which have in common the following:

  • they treat the use of paranormal concepts in wide-use technology as a mundane fact, accepted by the Foundation and subject to a high degree of categorisation and scientific analysis;
  • they work less as stories than as pure world-building - they have no characters of note, and don't particularly run from A to B - they are by way of background to the universe.

I don't hugely enjoy either of those things without something more - they reduce the wonder and terror of the Foundation universe, and the articles themselves are low-impact, as it's much harder to have a hook without the forward propulsion of a story.

This one even has a brand-new form of magic science in the Kardec counter, which I didn't like when it was called a Scranton Reality Anchor.

My point is: if ghosts and demons are so well-understood that they can be categorised by type, and used in household applications, then are they even anomalous anymore? Why not just leak the details to science (calling them waves or particles or some other non-emotive name) and stop wasting money containing them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm torn on the basic premise.

On the one hand, my knee-jerk reaction is that there's NO way the Foundation would be OK with ghost-powered lightbulbs being sold the public.

On the other hand, the Foundation has much bigger fish to fry, and saying "OK, fine, just so long as you stay away from the Factory & dangerous ghosts, and don't tell people it's anything paranormal" makes a certain amount of sense. And the Foundation are very pragmatic, so perhaps they would consider this route? Maybe? Especially since allowing it keeps it where they can see, and thus better monitor it.

I also love the irony of it being a completely mundane story about a light bulb manufacturer issuing a product recall after critically failing at basic vendor research, and the in-tone description of ectomorphs.

For the time being, no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is excellent.

Out-of-universe, it's basically world-building through implication.

Exactly. And it's damn well done!

I love the way you describe casual incidents (broken lightbulbs, product recall, problem between a manufacturer and subcontractor/supplier…) … in a story about ghosts.
And the science jargon about ghosts.
And the Foundation realistic enough not to bother if it gets its Kardec Counters.

From what ChrisBChikin said above:

You're trying to define and categorise ghosts and ectoplasm in an article about light bulbs, when really, ectoplasm alone should be an SCP all by itself. Really, the light bulbs aren't even anomalous; they're just ordinary glass and metal with one other material which is an anomaly. Putting light bulbs as the main topic forces you to focus on that and gloss over ghosts and the classification thereof.

IMO, that's the best aspect of the article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, there's stuff that I like here from a technical standpoint - tone and so on are good - but I have to say I'm not a fan.

I think my issue with this article can be boiled down to two main factors.

The first is that the Foundation's reaction to TCAE does not mesh with my own view of how the Foundation functions. I don't see them allowing TCAE to continue selling any anomalous products (and 'paratechnological' is just another word for anomalous). Think about it; the Foundation locks away all anomalies they possibly can, even when they might be benign, friendly, harmless or useful, because even the knowledge that anomalies exist is something too dangerous to be shared with the world at large. Why would they allow a company to package and sell anomalies when doing so makes a large scale security breach basically inevitable. If a product using previously unknown science (like paratechnology) hits the market, the first thing rival companies are going to do is take it apart and find out how it works so they can make a competing product. They find out the product is powered by ghosts; they find out ghosts are real; they find out anomalies are real and in that one instant every measure the Foundation has ever taken to maintain the secrecy of itself and its work comes crashing down around its ears. With this in mind, letting TCAE continue trading at all is too great a security risk to permit.

Secondly, I think the article tries to do too much and focuses on the wrong thing. You're trying to define and categorise ghosts and ectoplasm in an article about light bulbs, when really, ectoplasm alone should be an SCP all by itself. Really, the light bulbs aren't even anomalous; they're just ordinary glass and metal with one other material which is an anomaly. Putting light bulbs as the main topic forces you to focus on that and gloss over ghosts and the classification thereof. A better angle to attack this would be SCP-2176: Ectoplasm in which you can go into the mechanics of the substance, ghosts, and the classification system and the behaviours of each class. You can describe the sorts of 'anomalous effects' the ghosts cause, since you don't actually describe them in this article beyond saying they exist. The light bulbs themselves warrant maybe a single paragraph origin story beginning with something to the effect of, "The Foundation first became aware of SCP-2176 when it was discovered TCAE were using it to make light bulbs…"

So unfortunately it's a -1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For reference, and because I've seen this suggested in a few other places, there isn't really an attempt to create a new GOI happening here. If anything, I'd consider them just a subset of Promerheus Labs.

As for the rest of your criticisms/feedback… well, I obviously don't share them. I may try reordering the information, see if I like the new flow better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had a whole thing typed up, and than my phone browser reloaded.

The gist is that this reads like an intelligence report and case history loading down an anomaly that's kind of neat but not strong enough to carry all that weight. Yes, all of the background info flows logically if we adopt your suppositions, but that doesn't make it an interesting part of the narrative.

Also, the Factory felt like bad crosslinking, letting the generic evil GoI take some blame for the skip while also making then Lego with your new GoI.

On the immediate short term, I think it would strongly improve it if you moved the bit about the bulbs breaking to the top of the description. The background isn't going to be interesting without stakes, and you've buried the consequences.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rather silly idea (-1) but with appropriate tone for the article. (+1)

I think it'd be better as a tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Initially downvoted on a gut reaction to the vanilla idea, but the more I think about it the more I like the foundation's solution to the TCAE. Fits very well with my headcanon of the Foundation having a very pragmatic/Realpolitik approach to maintaining normalcy. That is to say, they know there are wizards and wereotters and basement mad scientists all over the place. They only care about the ones that start making a nuisance of themselves, or that are weird even by the foundation's standards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure this works, but I want to laud you for actually explaining what ectoplasm, etc., are in in-universe, clinical tone. It's much better than just dropping words like that into an article and expecting them to stick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Factory makes things stuff that have a some bigger/bloody price. That's the point, that's their thing. The only people who may be happy with their interaction with The Factory thusfar is some of the Church of the Broken God, and I don't think they'd exactly give glowing reviews. Either The Factory contacted them, in which case these people just said yes to a random unknown company giving them ghosts without looking into the supplier or looking at the supplies before jamming them into their products, or they somehow contacted The Factory, knowing what they are, and did all those things.

You're saying they just dove into this without looking into anything, they didn't test things, they just made a product so wonky that The Foundation had to step in to teach them how to sell anomalous stuff to the public.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually would love this if it was followed by one where they figured out how to get it to talk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Getting screwed over by a bad supplier isn't the same as incompetence. Poor quality control may be, but when the thing you'd be testing for shouldn't even be there in the first place, and you've been told it's not, it's understandable that they didn't think to check for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So we have a normal thing powered by ghosts. I'd say Diet Ghost did it better, to begin with, but that isn't my only issue with the article. You took some of the remnants of Prometheus Labs (the infamous incompetent GoI) and made them into… another incompetent GoI. Not only are they selling items that can cause bad shit to happen because they weren't tested properly, not only do they get caught, they worked with The Factory of all people as a distributor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, I didn't see that the first time, and now that I've reread it after reading that^ it does explain the dryness and the basic shape of the article.

Edit: Only later did the GoI actually made sense to me (dumb Gnago) so that made the article a lot better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've added some more details in footnotes that should give a relatively comprehensive-but-brief overview of all the ectoscience involved, and clarified wording in a few places to make it more clear that the lightbulbs work by electrocuting ghosts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like it when an SCP introduces a brand new GoI and does it well, and this is no exception.

Honestly, there really isn't anything to dislike here, except for maybe the brevity of the article, but even then, there's enough here to warrant an upvote out of me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get that, and that's why I find what's here good, but I feel like some additional description of this thing would make it a lot more well-rounded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed the way the Foundation managed this GoI.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I suppose my view of it is that the descriptions and definitions of all the ghost science don't belong in the article proper, except for a cursory explanation of certain important terms. The idea is to imply the existence of a larger field of science focused on studying ghosts. In that case, in-universe readers would be expected to know these already. Research papers about theoretical physics don't take time to explain the history of electrons, for example.

Out-of-universe, it's basically world-building through implication.

If enough people think this is an issue, I can throw in a paragraph with some more details.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Much of the dryness is intentional. It's not a super interesting anomaly, it doesn't do anything crazy. But it does give an insight into how the Foundation deals with anomalous items that don't pose enough of a threat to normalcy to justify expending anything more than a minimal amount of resources on.

Many of the GOIs, by their existence, imply that there is a semi-open market for anomalous items. My take on this is that the Foundation and the GOC allow some amount of open trading of anomalous items, as long as they don't threaten normalcy. This is easier than trying to clamp-down on the market, as it keeps things in a place where they can monitor.

What this is, in essence, is an investigatory report for a defective product. But instead of lead paint in children's toys, it's angry ghosts inside lightbulbs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See this is interesting, and I like it. But I feel like you wrote the history and recovery logs for an SCP instead of the SCP itself. Like, this is missing a description of the entities from these bulbs, maybe with some footnotes defining the ecto-what-have-you. I would upvote this if it were a full article as I like the idea and where this takes it, but this feels incomplete to me as it is now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Johnson: Have you always been a lightbulb, and have you always contained ectomorphs?
SCP-2176: …
Dr. Johnson: I see. Are you in any other way anomalous, aside from containing ectomorphs?
SCP-2176: …
Dr. Johnson: …
SCP-2176: …
Dr. Johnson: Thank you for your cooperation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As canaryfarmer said, it is rather dry. It doesn't have an interting enough anomaly to make it interesting on its own. I think it could be improved by adding something to the hitory of the factory (Although I could be wrong and that could go terribly) Although I do think it was well written and the idea is decent1, so a +1 from me. yay, the 2176 space got a decent article!

Footnotes
1. It can definitely be expanded on somehow IMO

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So they were putting gremlins in consumer products! That crazy snow plow guy was right!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like I said in chat, I dig it.

It adds to the Prometheus Labs mythology, dovetails nicely with AJMansfield's demonology tales, produces interesting and refreshing interactions between various anomalous players, and does so in a nice, compact package without trying to make the Foundation, say, interview the light bulbs.

Good work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm, the treatment towards TCAE is rather refreshing. To think that a GoI (albeit a lesser GoI) bothers to listen to the Foundation and follow through their protocols to ensure security, containment and protection.

Not all GoIs have to oppose the Foundation. The only real prerequisite is that they are of interest to the Foundation, likely due to possession of anomalies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno, it's just…too dry. "There were ghosts in some lights, and then there weren't." Nothing interesting, emotionally or narratively, ever happens.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Fact: Putting ghosts inside something automatically makes it 87% cooler.

Thanks to EmberTech, Some_Potato, ribby, A_Random_Day, TyGently, and l33tr for reviewing this while it was a draft. If I missed your name, give me a poke.

I may add a photo to this at a latter date, depending on whether I can get a CC photo of a fluorescent lightbulb still in its packaging. As it is though, I feel that it can stand on its own just fine without a photo.

Also, since a lot of people apparently missed this, let me clarify that these lightbulbs work by electrocuting ghosts. The reason the Class-A ectomorphs are a hazard is because they're pissed off about being tortured. The reason it's only the Class-A ectomorphs that are a hazard is because they're the only ones that have sapiency and agency, making them the only ones that can want, plan, and take revenge.
